title: Worksheet 5: Working with Text
description: Learn to use strings to work with text and create more expressive programs.
designNote: Worksheet 5 is a revision / breather worksheet. Introduce ONE new concept only (print with multiple comma-separated arguments), then use it as a fresh surface to revise prior concepts (variables, input, arithmetic, if/else, loops). Resist adding further new syntax — the job here is consolidation and confidence, not new load. The final section (modulo onwards) is an extension track for fast-finishing students, ending in a prime-number challenge.

problems.title: Hello World!
problems.content: Welcome back! So far, we've been working with numbers. Now let's learn to work with text. In programming, we call text strings. A string is text wrapped in quotation marks.
problems.task: Run this code to see your first string printed.
problems.hint: Just press the 'Run Code' button.

rules.solutionCode: print("Hello World")

problems.title: Print with commas
problems.content:
Up to now, every print() has shown one thing. But print() can actually take many things at once — just put commas between them:

name = 'Sam'
print('Hello', name)

Python prints each item in order with a space between them. That's it — no extra syntax, no quotes inside quotes.

It works with words, numbers, or a mix:

age = 10
print('I am', age, 'years old')

This comma-style print() is the one new idea in this worksheet. After that, we'll use it to revise everything you've learned so far — variables, input, if/else, and loops.
problems.footer: Put commas between items in print() — Python adds a space between each one.

problems.title: Watch print with commas in action
Meet print with commas
problems.content: Let's try it. Store a name in a variable, then print a greeting using a comma to mix text and the variable.
problems.task: Run this code to see how the variable name appears in the greeting.
problems.hint: Notice the comma between "Hello" and name. Python prints them both, with a space in between.

rules.solutionCode:
name = "Sam"
print("Hello", name)

problems.title: Change the greeting
problems.content: Now it's your turn to make a small change.
problems.task: Edit the greeting from "Hello" to "Hi" but keep the rest of the print the same.
problems.hint: Just change the word Hello to Hi. Don't touch the comma or the name variable.

rules.solutionCode:
name = "Sam"
print("Hi", name)

problems.title: Single quotes work too!
problems.content: So far we've used double quotes " for our strings. Python also lets you use single quotes ' - they work exactly the same.
problems.task: Run this code to see that single quotes work just like double quotes.
problems.hint: Both 'Hello World' and "Hello World" produce the same output.

rules.solutionCode: print('Hello World')

problems.title: Quote bug - mixing quotes!
problems.content:
This is a common mistake that everyone makes when learning Python! If you start a string with one type of quote, you must end it with the same type.

When you run the broken code below, Python says "unterminated string literal" — that's Python's way of saying "your string didn't finish properly — check the quotes match".
problems.task: Run this code to see the error. The string starts with a double quote " but ends with a single quote '. Fix it by making both quotes match.
problems.hint: Change the ending single quote ' to a double quote " so they match. Both quotes should be the same type.

rules.solutionCode: print("Welcome")

problems.title: Fix the bug: comma in the wrong place
problems.content: This program has a bug. The comma is in the wrong place — it's inside the quotes instead of separating the two things being printed. Python sees one long string "Hello," and then a bare name with nothing in between, and gets confused.
problems.task: Run the code to see the error. Move the comma so it's outside the quotes, separating the string from the variable.
problems.hint: Change "Hello," name to "Hello", name. The comma lives between the two things inside the print(), not inside the quotes.

rules.solutionCode:
name = "Sam"
print("Hello", name)

problems.title: Numbers with print
problems.content: Print with commas works with numbers too. You can mix words and numbers in a single print().
problems.task: Run this code to see how a number variable appears in the output.
problems.hint: The three pieces — "I am", age, "years old" — all get printed with spaces between them.

rules.solutionCode:
age = 10
print("I am", age, "years old")

problems.title: Fix the bug: missing commas
problems.content: Another bug. This program has no commas between the three things inside print(). Python needs those commas to know where one item ends and the next begins.
problems.task: Run the code to see the error. Add the two missing commas so the program prints I am 10 years old.
problems.hint: One comma goes between "I am" and age. The other goes between age and "years old".

rules.solutionCode:
age = 10
print("I am", age, "years old")

problems.title: Your name with print
problems.content: Now let's write a print-with-commas from scratch.
problems.task: Write a program that sets a variable called name to your name (in quotes), then prints "My name is" followed by name.
problems.hint: First line: name = "YourName". Second line: print("My name is", name).

rules.message: Create a variable called name with your name.
Use print with a comma to combine the text and your name variable.

problems.title: Loop reminder
problems.content: Remember loops from Worksheet 3? They help us repeat code.
problems.task: Run this loop to see the numbers 1 to 5 printed.
problems.hint: The loop runs 5 times, printing each number.

rules.solutionCode:
for i in range(1, 6):
  print(i)

problems.title: Loops meet print with commas
problems.content: Now let's combine loops with comma-print. This lets you label each number the loop produces.
problems.task: Write a loop that counts from 1 to 5. For each number, print "Number" followed by the current value of i.
problems.hint: Use: for i in range(1, 6): and then print("Number", i).

rules.message: Use a for loop with range().
Use print with a comma to combine "Number" and i.

rules.solutionCode:
for i in range(1, 6):
  print("Number", i)

problems.title: Fix the bug: capital letters matter
problems.content: Python is very picky about capital letters. name and Name are treated as two different variables — so if you spell a variable differently from when you made it, Python says it doesn't exist.
problems.task: Run the code to see the error: NameError: name 'Name' is not defined. Fix the typo so both lines use the same lowercase spelling.
problems.hint: Change Name on line 2 to name (with a lowercase n), to match the variable on line 1.

rules.solutionCode:
name = "Sam"
print("Hello", name)

problems.title: Age with input
problems.content: Let's combine print-with-commas with user input. We've already filled in the get_input() line for you — just add the print.
problems.task: The first line stores the entered number in a variable called age. Add a second line that prints "I am" followed by age.
problems.hint: Add: print("I am", age) on the second line.

rules.message: Use get_input() to get the age.
Use print with a comma to combine the text and the age variable.

problems.title: If statement reminder
problems.content: Remember if statements from Worksheet 4? They let our programs make decisions based on conditions.
problems.task: Run this code. Try entering different numbers like 30, then 60, and see how the output changes.
problems.hint: When score is 50 or more, it prints 'Pass'. Otherwise, nothing is printed.

problems.title: Fix the bug: missing colon
problems.content: Remember from Worksheet 4 — every if line must end with a colon :. Without it, Python doesn't know the if block has started, and shows a syntax error.
problems.task: Run the code to see the error. Add the missing : at the end of the if line to fix it.
problems.hint: Put a : at the very end of the if score >= 50 line.

problems.title: Add an else block
problems.content: The code above only prints something when the condition is true. Let's make it print something for both cases.
problems.task: Add an else: block after the if block that prints "Try again". Test it with different score values!
problems.hint: After the print("Pass") line, add: else: on a new line (at the same indentation as if), then print("Try again") indented under it.

problems.title: Fix the bug: = vs ==
problems.content: Another classic from Worksheet 4. A single = means "store a value in a variable" (assignment). A double == asks "are these equal?" (comparison). They are not the same, and mixing them up inside an if causes a syntax error.
problems.task: Run the code to see the error. Fix the bug by changing the single = inside the if to a double ==.
problems.hint: The n = on the first line is fine — that's storing a value. The bug is the n = 10 inside the if — change that single = to ==.

problems.title: If statements with print
problems.content: Now let's combine if statements with print-with-commas to make more interesting messages.
problems.task: Complete the program: If n is greater than 10, print n followed by "is big". Otherwise, print n followed by "is small".
problems.hint: You need an if/else. Inside each branch, use print() with two things separated by a comma — the variable n and a message string.

rules.message: Use get_input() to get the number.
Use an if/else statement to check the number.
Use print with a comma to combine n and your message.

problems.title: Greeting with input
problems.content: Now let's get a name from the user and create a personalized greeting.
problems.task: Write a program that gets a name input and prints "Welcome" followed by the name. Note: For text inputs, you still use get_input() the same way.
problems.hint: Get the input: name = get_input(). Then print with a comma: print("Welcome", name).

rules.message: Use get_input() to get the name.
Use print with a comma to combine "Welcome" and the name.

rules.solutionCode:
name = get_input()
print("Welcome", name)

problems.title: Full introduction
problems.content: Now let's use lots of things in one print. When a problem has two input boxes, we tell get_input() which one to read by putting the input's name in quotes inside the brackets — like get_input('name') on the first line.
problems.task: The first line reads the name from its input box. Add a second line that reads the age the same way, then a third line that prints: "My name is", name, "and I am", age.
problems.hint: Second line: age = get_input('age') — same pattern as the first line. Third line: print("My name is", name, "and I am", age).

rules.message: Use get_input('name') and get_input('age') to get both inputs.
Your print() should have four comma-separated parts.

rules.solutionCode:
name = get_input('name')
age = get_input('age')
print("My name is", name, "and I am", age)

problems.title: Three greetings
problems.content: Let's create a program that prints multiple different messages with the same name.
problems.task: Get a name as input, then print three different greetings: "Hello", name, then "Welcome", name, then "Goodbye", name.
problems.hint: Get the input once, then use three separate print statements, each with a comma.

rules.solutionCode:
name = get_input()
print("Hello", name)
print("Welcome", name)
print("Goodbye", name)

problems.title: Extension: Modulo %
problems.content:
From here you'll meet a new math symbol and use it to solve some tricky puzzles — even checking whether a number is prime!

Python has a symbol called modulo — it looks like a percent sign %. It gives you the remainder of a division.

Imagine sharing 10 sweets between 3 friends: each friend gets 3, and 1 is left over. That leftover is what % gives you:

print(10 % 3)   # 1
print(14 % 4)   # 2
print(9 % 3)    # 0 — no remainder, 3 divides 9 evenly

It sounds small, but % unlocks surprising powers — checking even/odd, finding factors, and detecting prime numbers.

problems.title: Finding the Remainder %
problems.content: Time to try the modulo operator yourself! The percent sign % gives the remainder after division.
problems.task: Run the code to see the remainder of 14 divided by 4. Change the numbers to see how it works with different values.
problems.hint: 14 divided by 4 is 3 with a remainder of 2.

problems.title: Even or Odd?
problems.content: We can use the modulo operator % to see if a number is even or odd. Any even number can be divided by 2 with no remainder! So, if num % 2 is 0, the number is even.
problems.task: Enter different numbers, both even and odd, and run the code to see if the program gets it right.
problems.hint: Try entering 10, then try 5. See how the result of num % 2 changes the outcome.

problems.title: Even or odd with print
problems.content: Now let's make the even/odd checker more descriptive by using comma-print. Edit the print statements to include the number in the message.
problems.task: Change both print statements to include the number. Instead of "Even", print num followed by "is even". Instead of "Odd", print num followed by "is odd".
problems.hint: Change print("Even") to print(num, "is even") and print("Odd") to print(num, "is odd").

rules.message: Use == to check if the remainder is 0.
Use print with a comma to combine num and your message.

problems.title: Reminder: Indentation Inside a Loop
problems.content:
When you put an if inside a for loop, you need two levels of indentation. Each level adds 2 spaces:

for i in range(3):          # level 0
  if i == 1:               # level 1 — 2 spaces
    print(i)              # level 2 — 4 spaces

The if is indented because it's inside the loop. The print is indented again because it's inside both the loop and the if.
problems.footer: Rule: every time you go one level deeper, add 2 more spaces.

problems.title: Odd numbers in a loop
problems.content: Let's combine loops with the modulo check. Instead of checking one number at a time, you can loop through many and only print the ones you want.
problems.task: Write a loop that goes through the numbers 1 to 20 and only prints the odd ones. Use % 2 to check each one.
problems.hint: Inside the loop, use if i % 2 != 0: (remainder is not 0 means odd). Then print(i) indented under the if. Remember: that's two levels of indent — 4 spaces on the print line.

rules.message: Use a for loop with range().
Use an if statement inside the loop.
Use % 2 to check if the number is odd.

problems.title: Finding Factors
problems.content: A factor of a number is any number that divides into it evenly (with no remainder). For example, the factors of 12 are 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, and 12. We can use modulo to check if a number is a factor!
problems.task: This program loops through numbers 1 to n. Edit the code to only print i when it's a factor of n (when n % i == 0). Add an if statement inside the loop.
problems.starterCode:
n = get_input()
print("Factors of", n)

for i in range(1, n + 1):
  print(i)
problems.hint: Add if n % i == 0: after the for loop line, then indent the print(i) underneath it by 4 spaces total. This checks if i divides evenly into n.

problems.title: Building a prime checker
problems.content:
A prime number has exactly two factors: 1 and itself. Examples: 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13. Numbers that aren't prime are called composite — they have more factors. For example, 12 has factors 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12.

How could a program check if a number is prime? It can look for factors — and the moment it finds one, it knows the number is composite. It doesn't need to keep checking after that.

The next few problems give you the two tools you need:

  • break — stops a loop early, as soon as you have your answer
  • flag variable — a True/False variable that remembers what you found

At the end, you'll put them together in the final challenge: decide if a number is prime or composite.
problems.footer: A prime number has exactly 2 factors: 1 and itself.

problems.title: The break keyword
problems.content: Sometimes you want to exit a loop early before it finishes all its iterations. The break keyword stops a loop immediately and jumps to the code after the loop.
problems.task: Run this code and see how the loop stops at 3 instead of going all the way to 10.
problems.hint: The break happens when i equals 3, so it only prints 1, 2, 3 before exiting the loop.

problems.title: Your turn: Add a break
problems.content: Now practice adding break yourself. This loop counts from 1 to 20, but let's make it stop early.
problems.task: Add an if statement inside the loop that breaks when i equals 7. Don't forget to add break inside the if block!
problems.hint: After the print(i) line, add: if i == 7: and then on the next line (indented): break

problems.title: Finding with a flag variable
problems.content: Sometimes we need to track whether we found something in a loop. We can use a flag variable - a variable that starts as False and becomes True when we find what we're looking for.
problems.task: This program has a break but doesn't set the flag! Add found = True before the break statement, so the program remembers that it found something.
problems.hint: Add 'found = True' on the line before 'break'. The flag variable tracks whether we found a number greater than target.

problems.title: Challenge: Prime or Composite?
problems.content: A prime number has exactly two factors: 1 and itself. A composite number has more than two factors. Use what you've learned about factors, the break keyword, and flag variables to decide.
problems.task: Write a program that reads a number (try numbers 2 or bigger) and prints "Prime" if it's prime or "Composite" if it's not. Use a flag variable like in the previous question!
problems.hint: Start with a flag variable set to False. Loop from 2 to n-1. If you find a factor (n % i == 0), set the flag to True and break. After the loop, check the flag to print either 'Prime' or 'Composite'.
